turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion of the Court in two cases this morning.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: The first case in which there is an opinion for the Court is Packingham versus North Carolina. In 2008, North Carolina enacted a statute making it a felony for a registered sex offender to gain access to certain websites. The statute covers, and these are the statutory words; commercial, social networking websites, where the sex offender knows that the site permits minor children to become members or to create or maintain personal web pages. Now that definition includes at a minimum websites like Facebook and Twitter. In this case, the court considers whether that law is permissible, the First Amendment's free speech clause applicable to the states under the Fourteenth Amendment. In 2002, petitioner, Lester Gerard Packingham pleaded guilty to indecent liberties with a minor. He was required to register as a sex offender and that status can endure for 30 years or more. Eight years later, a state court dismissed a traffic ticket against the petitioner. In response, petitioner logged on to Facebook.com and posted a brief statement on his personal profile celebrating the dismissal of the traffic ticket. He was convicted of violating the North Carolina statute. The Court of Appeals of North Carolina, struck down the statute on First Amendment grounds, but the North Carolina Supreme Court reversed. The State Supreme Court ruling upheld the statute. A fundamental principle of the First Amendment is that all persons have access to places where they can listen and speak, and then after reflection speak and listened once more. Cyberspace including social media is one of the most important places for these activities. Seven in ten American adults use at least one Internet social networking service. These websites offer relatively unlimited low cost capacity communications of all kind. On Facebook, for example, users can debate religion or politics with their friends and neighbors. On LinkedIn, users can advertise for employees, and on Twitter users can petition their elected representative and otherwise engage with them in direct and indirect manner. The cyber age may be a revolution of historic proportions, but we cannot appreciate yet its full dimensions and that's potential to alter how we express ourselves. As a result, the court must exercise extreme caution before suggesting that the first amendment provides scant protection for access to vast networks in that medium. This background informs the analysis of the North Carolina law that is at issue here. Even assuming that that statute is subject to intermediate scrutiny it cannot stand because it is not sufficiently narrowly tailored to serve a significant government interest. A legislature may of course pass valid laws to protect children and other victims of sexual assault from abuse, but the assertion of a valid governmental interest cannot in every context be insulated from all constitutional protections. It's necessary to make two assumptions to resolve this case. First, as the state concedes, the law applies at least to social networking websites as commonly understood that is websites like Facebook, LinkedIn and Twitter. Second, although the issue is not before the court, it can be assumed that the first amendment does permit a state to enact narrowly tailored laws that prohibit a sex offender from engaging in conduct but often presages a sexual crime. Even with these assumptions, the statute here enacts a prohibition unprecedented in the scope of the First Amendment activity it burdens. By barring sex offenders from using these websites, North Carolina prohibits access to what for many are the principle sources for exploring the vast realms of human thought and knowledge. Such websites are, as well, perhaps the most powerful mechanisms available to a private citizen to make his or her voice heard. It is well established that as a general rule, the government may not suppress lawful speech as the means to suppress unlawful speech. That is what the North Carolina law has done here and this law must be held invalid. The judgment of the North Carolina Supreme Court is reversed. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, in which the Chief Justice and Justice Thomas join. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
